title: Thymus teucrioides subsp. candilicus
what_description: Thymus teucrioides subsp. candilicus is a subspecies of flowering plant in the family Lamiaceae, native to the Iberian Peninsula. It is a perennial herbaceous plant with a woody base, 10-25 cm tall, and a branched, leafy stem. The leaves are lanceolate to elliptical in shape, 1-2.5 cm long, and 2-8 mm wide, with a greyish-green color and a wrinkled surface. The flowers are pinkish-purple in color, clustered in axillary whorls, and 6-8 mm long, with a four-lobed corolla. The calyx is campanulate, 4-6 mm long, and has four unequal lobes.
ailments_description: The most common ailments you can heal with Thymus teucrioides subsp. candilicus are digestive issues, such as diarrhea, and gastroenteritis, due to its antispasmodic and anti-inflammatory properties, as well as its ability to regulate bowel movements and reduce inflammation in the gastrointestinal tract. This plant also exhibits antimicrobial properties, making it effective in treating fungal infections, bacterial infections, and wound infections, thereby aiding in wound healing and reducing the risk of skin infections and urinary tract infections. Additionally, its anti-inflammatory properties help alleviate respiratory issues, such as cough and respiratory infections, by reducing inflammation in the airways and relieving congestion. Furthermore, the plant's antiseptic and anti-inflammatory properties make it an effective treatment for skin irritations, fever, and sore throat, while also reducing inflammation in wounds and promoting wound healing.
properties_description: The main therapeutic properties of Thymus teucrioides subsp. candilicus are attributed to its bioactive compounds, which possess significant antimicrobial activity, providing a natural defense against harmful microorganisms. Additionally, this plant exhibits antioxidant properties, effectively neutralizing free radicals and protecting against oxidative stress. Its anti-inflammatory properties make it a potential remedy for alleviating pain and discomfort associated with various inflammatory conditions. Furthermore, Thymus teucrioides subsp. candilicus has been found to exhibit antifungal properties, inhibiting the growth of fungal pathogens and preventing infections. The bioactive compounds responsible for these medicinal properties include flavonoids, saponins, and volatile oils, such as thymol and carvacrol, which contribute to the plant's antibacterial activity, allowing it to combat bacterial infections effectively.
constituents_description: The most important healing constituents of Thymus teucrioides subsp. candilicus are thymol, a potent antimicrobial agent with broad-spectrum activity against bacteria, viruses, and fungi, carvacrol, another antimicrobial compound with antiviral and antioxidant properties, linalool, a terpene with anti-inflammatory and sedative effects, rosmarinic acid, a phenolic diterpene with antioxidant and anti-inflammatory activities, and bornyl acetate, a sesquiterpene with analgesic and sedative properties. Thymol's mechanism of action involves disrupting cell membranes and interfering with protein synthesis, while carvacrol's antiviral activity is attributed to its ability to inhibit viral replication. Linalool's anti-inflammatory effects are mediated by its ability to inhibit pro-inflammatory enzymes and cytokines. Rosmarinic acid's antioxidant activity is due to its ability to scavenge free radicals and chelate metal ions, while bornyl acetate's analgesic effects are thought to be mediated by its ability to inhibit pain signaling pathways. These constituents contribute to the plant's overall therapeutic potential, making it a valuable resource for the development of new medicines.
preparations_description: The main herbal preparations of Thymus teucrioides subsp. candilicus are tea, infusion, tincture, salve, and thyme tea, which are typically derived from the leaves and flowers of the plant. Tea and infusion are usually prepared by steeping the plant material in hot water to release its bioactive compounds, such as flavonoids and phenolic acids, which have been shown to exhibit antioxidant and antimicrobial properties. Tincture is a concentrated liquid extract of the plant, often used in pharmaceutical and herbal remedies, which can be prepared using a solvent like ethanol or glycerin. Salve is a topical preparation made by infusing the plant material in a carrier oil, such as olive or coconut oil, to create a soothing and antimicrobial ointment for skin conditions. Thyme tea, a specific preparation made from Thymus teucrioides subsp. candilicus, has been traditionally used to aid digestion and relieve respiratory issues due to its carminative and expectorant properties.
side_effects_description: The possible side effects of using Thymus teucrioides subsp. candilicus improperly include experience allergic reactions due to its chemical composition, such as terpenoids and flavonoids, which may trigger an immune response in some individuals. Additionally, developing skin irritation is a possible side effect, as the plant's essential oil can cause contact dermatitis and other skin reactions. Nausea and vomiting may also occur due to the plant's toxic compounds, which can irritate the stomach and gastrointestinal tract. Furthermore, headaches and other central nervous system symptoms can be triggered by the plant's ability to cause histamine release, leading to vasodilation and other neurological effects. These side effects can be severe and potentially life-threatening, especially in individuals with pre-existing medical conditions or when used in excessive amounts.
